iterations.theme: Eshatološke besjede: Upozorenja, progoni i razorenje Jeruzalema

sections.subtitle: Uvod u eshatološke besjede: Dramatična upozorenja iz Markova evanđelja
sections.screenshot_description: Voditelj uvodi u temu eshatoloških besjeda iz Markova evanđelja, s naglaskom na dramatične najave.
sections.content:
Voditelj uvodi u trinaesto poglavlje Markova evanđelja, ističući njegovu eshatološku prirodu i dramatične teme koje obrađuje. Uspoređuje Isusova upozorenja s osobnim iskustvom iz osnovne škole, gdje je profesor dramatično najavio 'suze i plač' ako se ne bude učilo, što je tadašnjim učenicima djelovalo kao 'kraj svijeta'.

Ovo poglavlje, kako objašnjava, govori o 'grozoti pustoši koja stoluje gdje joj nije mjesto', o ratovima i glasinama o ratovima, potresima i gladi. Isus također najavljuje progone, predaju vijećima i sinagogama, te izdaju unutar obitelji. Voditelj pita Frantu o dubljem značenju ovih 'dramatičnih i mračnih predviđanja'. Frante objašnjava da Isusov govor počinje izlaskom iz Hrama, nakon dugog dana rasprava i oštrih riječi upućenih pismoznancima, te pohvale siromašnoj udovici. Izlazeći iz Hrama i promatrajući ga s Maslinske gore, učenici se dive njegovoj ljepoti i raskoši.
sections.subtitle_en: Introduction to eschatological discourses: Dramatic warnings from Mark's Gospel
sections.screenshot_description_en: The host introduces the topic of eschatological discourses from Mark's Gospel, with an emphasis on dramatic announcements.
sections.content_en: The host introduces the thirteenth chapter of Mark's Gospel, emphasizing its eschatological nature and the dramatic themes it addresses. He compares Jesus' warnings with a personal experience from elementary school, where the teacher dramatically announced 'tears and weeping' if they didn't study, which then seemed to the students like 'the end of the world'. This chapter, as he explains, speaks of 'the abomination of desolation standing where it ought not to be', of wars and rumors of wars, earthquakes and famine. Jesus also announces persecutions, being handed over to councils and synagogues, and betrayal within families. The host asks Franta about the deeper meaning of these 'dramatic and dark predictions'. Franta explains that Jesus' discourse begins with leaving the Temple, after a long day of discussions and sharp words addressed to the scribes, and praise for the poor widow. Leaving the Temple and observing it from the Mount of Olives, the disciples admire its beauty and splendor.

sections.subtitle: Isusovo proročanstvo o razorenju Hrama: Šok za učenike
sections.screenshot_description: Učenici s divljenjem gledaju Hram, dok Isus najavljuje njegovo razorenje.
sections.content:
Dok učenici s divljenjem promatraju Hram, jedan od njih uzvikuje: 'Učitelju, gledaj kakva li kamenja, kakva li zdanja!' Isus, svjestan veličanstvenosti Hrama, izgovara proročanstvo koje nitko od njih ne očekuje: 'Neće se ostaviti ni kamen na kamenu nerazvaljen.'

Frante naglašava važnost razumijevanja perspektive učenika. Za njih je Hram bio više od građevine; to je bilo mjesto gdje Bog boravi, središte svijeta, 'pupak svijeta'. Ideja o njegovom uništenju bila je nezamisliva, jer bi to značilo nestanak cijelog sustava i temelja njihove vjere. Učenici su vjerovali da bez Hrama život nije moguć, jer je on osiguravao središte oko kojeg se gradio njihov život i identitet. Isusovo proročanstvo stoga nije govorilo samo o fizičkom uništenju Hrama, već o rušenju cijelog sustava i predožbe života, uspostavljajući opreku između starog Hrama i novog Hrama, odnosno samog Isusa i njegovog tijela kao novog središta.
sections.subtitle_en: Jesus's prophecy about the destruction of the Temple: Shock for the disciples
sections.screenshot_description_en: The disciples look with admiration at the Temple, while Jesus announces its destruction.
sections.content_en: While the disciples observe the Temple with admiration, one of them exclaims: 'Teacher, look what stones, what buildings!' Jesus, aware of the Temple's magnificence, utters a prophecy that none of them expect: 'Not one stone will be left upon another, unthrown down.' Frante emphasizes the importance of understanding the disciples' perspective. For them, the Temple was more than a building; it was the place where God dwells, the center of the world, 'the navel of the world'. The idea of its destruction was unimaginable, because it would mean the disappearance of the entire system and the foundations of their faith. The disciples believed that without the Temple, life was not possible, because it provided the center around which their life and identity were built. Jesus' prophecy therefore spoke not only of the physical destruction of the Temple, but of the demolition of the entire system and conception of life, establishing an opposition between the old Temple and the new Temple, that is, Jesus himself and his body as the new center.

sections.subtitle: Učenici traže znakove: Početak Isusovih eshatoloških govora
sections.screenshot_description: Petar, Jakov, Ivan i Andrija pitaju Isusa o vremenu i znakovima razorenja.
sections.content:
Voditelj ističe da je Isusovo proročanstvo o razorenju Hrama moralo biti izuzetno teško za učenike, lomeći njihove dotadašnje predodžbe. Frante potvrđuje da se to proročanstvo doslovno ispunilo 70. godine poslije Krista. Nakon Isusovih riječi, četvorica učenika – Petar, Jakov, Ivan i Andrija – prilaze mu nasamo i pitaju: 'Recite nam, kad će to biti i na koji se znak sve to ima srušiti?' Njihovo pitanje proizlazi iz želje da se pripreme za ono što doživljavaju kao najavu apokalipse i nestanka cijelog svijeta.

Ovim pitanjem započinje Isusov najduži govor u Markovu evanđelju, koji se proteže do kraja trinaestog poglavlja. Frante objašnjava da se prvi dio tog govora fokusira na 'početak nevolja', što Voditelj komentira kao poglavlje puno nedaća. Voditelj također povlači paralelu s praksom starih crkava, gdje su se na izlazu nalazila upozorenja, sugerirajući da je i Isusov govor, izrečen nakon izlaska iz Hrama, imao sličnu psihološku funkciju – pripremiti ljude za ono što dolazi.
sections.subtitle_en: Disciples seek signs: Beginning of Jesus's eschatological discourses
sections.screenshot_description_en: Peter, James, John, and Andrew ask Jesus about the time and signs of destruction.
sections.content_en: The host points out that Jesus' prophecy about the destruction of the Temple must have been extremely difficult for the disciples, breaking their previous notions. Frante confirms that that prophecy was literally fulfilled in the year 70 AD. After Jesus' words, four disciples – Peter, James, John, and Andrew – approach him privately and ask: 'Tell us, when will this be and by what sign is all this to be destroyed?' Their question stems from a desire to prepare themselves for what they perceive as an announcement of the apocalypse and the disappearance of the entire world. With this question begins Jesus' longest discourse in Mark's Gospel, which extends to the end of the thirteenth chapter. Frante explains that the first part of that discourse focuses on 'the beginning of sorrows,' which the host comments on as a chapter full of misfortunes. The host also draws a parallel with the practice of old churches, where warnings were placed at the exit, suggesting that Jesus' discourse, uttered after leaving the Temple, also had a similar psychological function – to prepare people for what is to come.

sections.subtitle: Upozorenja na zavodnike, ratove i katastrofe: 'Početak trudova'
sections.screenshot_description: Isus upozorava učenike na opasnosti lažnih proroka i zavodnika.
sections.content:
Frante objašnjava da izlazak iz Hrama simbolizira i odvajanje od institucije koja je postala neprijateljski nastrojena prema Isusu. To neprijateljstvo će rasti, dovodeći do progona i ubojstava učenika, poput Stjepana i Jakova. Isus najavljuje dolazak zavodnika, upozoravajući: 'Pazite da vas tko ne zavede.' Zavodnici su opisani kao opasni ljudi koji dolaze u lijepom ruhu, slatkorječivi, obećavaju najbolje, ali im je cilj odvesti ljude na stramputicu. Oni će govoriti u Isusovo ime, pozivajući se na njegov autoritet, što otežava razlikovanje pravih od lažnih proroka i svećenika. Voditelj dodaje da je ovo upozorenje relevantno i danas, pozivajući na oprez i aktivno promišljanje, čak i kada govore ljudi u svećeničkoj odjeći.

Nakon upozorenja na zavodnike, Isus govori o ratovima i glasinama o ratovima: 'Nemojte se uznemiravati, treba da se to dogodi, ali to još nije sve.' Frante pojašnjava da Marko ne govori o kozmičkoj apokalipsi, već o 'maloj apokalipsi' koja se tiče židovskog svijeta i njegovog ustroja. Vrijeme pisanja Markova evanđelja (60-te godine) već je bilo obilježeno progonima, što je stvorilo apokaliptično ozračje. Isusova upozorenja na 'narod protiv naroda, kraljevstvo protiv kraljevstva, potrese i glad' opisana su kao 'početak trudova', što znači da su to samo uvod u veće nevolje. Frante ističe da su to različite razine zala: od zavodnika unutar zajednice, preko ratova, do gladi kao najteže posljedice ratova.
sections.subtitle_en: Warnings about deceivers, wars, and catastrophes: 'Beginning of labor pains'
sections.screenshot_description_en: Jesus warns the disciples of the dangers of false prophets and seducers.
sections.content_en: Frante explains that leaving the Temple symbolizes also separation from the institution that had become hostile towards Jesus. That hostility will grow, leading to persecution and murders of disciples, like Stephen and James. Jesus announces the coming of deceivers, warning: 'Watch out that no one deceives you.' Deceivers are described as dangerous people who come in beautiful attire, sweet-talking, promising the best, but their goal is to lead people astray. They will speak in Jesus' name, invoking his authority, which makes it difficult to distinguish true from false prophets and priests. The host adds that this warning is relevant even today, calling for caution and active reflection, even when people in priestly attire speak. After the warning about deceivers, Jesus speaks about wars and rumors of wars: 'Do not be alarmed, it must happen, but that is not yet the end.' Frante clarifies that Mark does not speak about a cosmic apocalypse, but about a 'small apocalypse' concerning the Jewish world and its structure. The time of writing Mark's Gospel (the 60s) was already marked by persecutions, which created an apocalyptic atmosphere. Jesus' warnings about 'nation against nation, kingdom against kingdom, earthquakes and famine' are described as 'the beginning of birth pains,' meaning that these are only an introduction to greater troubles. Frante points out that these are different levels of evils: from deceivers within the community, through wars, to famine as the most severe consequence of wars.

sections.subtitle: Progonstva kao prilika za naviještanje Evanđelja i snaga unutarnjeg mira
sections.screenshot_description: Osoba koja propovijeda Evanđelje usred teških uvjeta i progona.
sections.content:
Frante objašnjava da Isus progonstva ne predstavlja kao patnju radi patnje, već kao priliku za naviještanje Evanđelja. Učenici će biti predavani vijećima i upraviteljima, ali će te nevolje poslužiti kao prigoda za širenje radosne vijesti. Raspršenje iz Jeruzalema, koje će uslijediti, bit će ispunjenje Isusove zapovijedi: 'Idite po svem svijetu i propovijedajte evanđelje.'

Voditelj postavlja pitanje o Isusovoj uputi 'pazite sami na sebe' u vremenima kaosa, rata i gladi, kada kršćani često osjećaju nemoć da riješe globalne probleme. Frante pojašnjava da zadaća učenika nije biti diplomat i uspostaviti mir među narodima, već se fokusirati na sebe, koristiti nevolje za naviještanje Evanđelja i sačuvati unutarnji mir. Vjeruje se da će Duh Sveti dati pravu riječ u pravo vrijeme. Kroz povijest su mnogi ljudi u teškim trenucima iskusili unutarnju snagu i jasnoću riječi, koja proizlazi iz odnosa s Bogom, a ne iz naučenih priča. Voditelj primjećuje da su vjernici, gledano politički i povijesno, često 'gubitnici', ali cilj kršćanstva nije uspostava političke strukture, već duhovnost. Frante se slaže, uspoređujući Isusov put do križa s 'gubitničkom pričom', ali ističe da Bog ima druge putove i da povijest vodi on, čak i tamo gdje nema političkog uspjeha. Pravi pobjednici nisu vojskovođe poput Aleksandra Velikog ili Napoleona, već oni koji, poput siromašne udovice, daju sve od sebe.
sections.subtitle_en: Persecutions as an opportunity for proclaiming the Gospel and the strength of inner peace
sections.screenshot_description_en: A person who preaches the Gospel amidst difficult conditions and persecution.
sections.content_en: Frante explains that Jesus does not present persecutions as suffering for the sake of suffering, but as an opportunity for proclaiming the Gospel. Disciples will be handed over to councils and governors, but these troubles will serve as an occasion for spreading the good news. The dispersion from Jerusalem, which will follow, will be the fulfillment of Jesus' command: 'Go into all the world and preach the Gospel.' The host asks a question about Jesus' instruction 'watch out for yourselves' in times of chaos, war, and famine, when Christians often feel powerless to solve global problems. Frante clarifies that the task of disciples is not to be a diplomat and establish peace among nations, but to focus on themselves, use troubles for proclaiming the Gospel, and preserve inner peace. It is believed that the Holy Spirit will give the right word at the right time. Throughout history, many people in difficult moments have experienced inner strength and clarity of words, which stems from a relationship with God, and not from learned stories. The host notes that believers, viewed politically and historically, are often 'losers', but the goal of Christianity is not the establishment of a political structure, but spirituality. Frante agrees, comparing Jesus' path to the cross with a 'loser's story', but emphasizes that God has other ways and that He leads history, even where there is no political success. The true victors are not military leaders like Alexander the Great or Napoleon, but those who, like the poor widow, give their all.

sections.subtitle: Razorenje Jeruzalema 70. godine: Povijesno ispunjenje proročanstva
sections.screenshot_description: Prikaz opsade Jeruzalema od strane rimskih legija, s Hramom u pozadini.
sections.content:
Frante ističe propast Jeruzalema kao ključnu temu, naglašavajući da su rani vjernici u tom događaju vidjeli jasno ispunjenje Isusovog proročanstva. Jeruzalem je razoren 70. godine poslije Krista, a o tome svjedoči očevidac, židovski povjesničar Josip Flavije. On se borio protiv Rimljana tijekom židovskog ustanka 66. godine, ali se predao i prorokovao Vespazijanu, tadašnjem rimskom vojskovođi, da će postati car. Vespazijan ga je poštedio i uzeo u službu. Kada je Vespazijan postao car i otišao u Rim, njegov sin Tit preuzeo je vojsku i opsadu Jeruzalema. Josip Flavije je bio prisutan i detaljno je opisao događaje u svom djelu 'Židovski rat'.

Zanimljivo je da kršćani nisu bili prisutni u opkoljenom Jeruzalemu. Euzebije Cezarejski, crkveni otac, navodi da su kršćani, po objavljenom proroštvu, napustili grad prije opsade i nastanili se u Peli, gradu u Pereji. Time su izbjegli masakr, a Euzebije to tumači kao Božju pravdu koja je pogodila one koji su se protivili Kristu i njegovim apostolima. On navodi brojna zla koja su se dogodila Židovima, fokusirajući se na glad, jedno od najstrašnijih iskustava opsade.
sections.subtitle_en: Destruction of Jerusalem in the year 70: Historical fulfillment of prophecy
sections.screenshot_description_en: Depiction of the siege of Jerusalem by Roman legions, with the Temple in the background.
sections.content_en: Frante highlights the fall of Jerusalem as a key theme, emphasizing that early believers saw in that event a clear fulfillment of Jesus' prophecy. Jerusalem was destroyed in 70 AD, and an eyewitness, the Jewish historian Josephus Flavius, testifies to this. He fought against the Romans during the Jewish revolt in 66 AD, but surrendered and prophesied to Vespasian, the then Roman general, that he would become emperor. Vespasian spared him and took him into service. When Vespasian became emperor and went to Rome, his son Titus took over the army and the siege of Jerusalem. Josephus Flavius was present and described the events in detail in his work 'The Jewish War'. It is interesting that Christians were not present in besieged Jerusalem. Eusebius of Caesarea, a Church Father, states that Christians, according to a revealed prophecy, left the city before the siege and settled in Pella, a city in Perea. Thus they avoided the massacre, and Eusebius interprets this as God's justice which struck those who opposed Christ and his apostles. He lists numerous evils that befell the Jews, focusing on famine, one of the most terrible experiences of the siege.

sections.subtitle: Glad i zvjerstva: Apokaliptička slika opsade Jeruzalema
sections.screenshot_description: Prikaz očaja i patnje ljudi u opkoljenom Jeruzalemu, s naglaskom na glad.
sections.content:
Frante detaljno opisuje strahote gladi tijekom opsade Jeruzalema, pozivajući se na svjedočanstva Josipa Flavija i Euzebija, te suvremenog autora Simona Sebaga Montefiorea i njegovu knjigu 'Jeruzalem biografija'. Opsadno stanje dovelo je do potpunog nedostatka hrane, žita i životinja. Glad je bila toliko ekstremna da su snažniji građani, naoružani, upadali u kuće i otimali hranu. Euzebije čak spominje grozan slučaj majke koja je, u očaju, ubila i pojela dio vlastitog djeteta, a miris pečenog mesa privukao je gladne koji su joj htjeli oteti i ostatak.

Ova zvjerstva su zgrožavala čak i Rimljane. Mrtvi su ležali nepokopani na krovovima kuća, stvarajući užasan zadah u gradu – istinska apokaliptička slika. Iako je Josip Flavije pokušavao uvjeriti Židove da se predaju, oni su se nadali otporu. Kada su Rimljani napokon probili zidine, Tit, rimski vojskovođa, navodno nije želio spaliti Hram, ali je jedan vojnik bacio baklju koja je zahvatila unutrašnjost. Hram je razoren do temelja, a srebro i zlato su opljačkani. Slavoluk u Rimu i danas svjedoči o tom događaju prikazujući menoru ukradenu iz Hrama. Zanimljivo je da je Hram razoren na isti dan kao i prvi Hram, koji su uništili Babilonci.
sections.subtitle_en: Hunger and atrocities: Apocalyptic image of the siege of Jerusalem
sections.screenshot_description_en: Depiction of the despair and suffering of people in besieged Jerusalem, with an emphasis on hunger.
sections.content_en: Frante describes in detail the horrors of famine during the siege of Jerusalem, referring to the testimonies of Josephus Flavius and Eusebius, and the contemporary author Simon Sebag Montefiore and his book 'Jerusalem: The Biography'. The state of siege led to a complete lack of food, grain, and animals. The hunger was so extreme that stronger citizens, armed, broke into houses and stole food. Eusebius even mentions a gruesome case of a mother who, in despair, killed and ate a part of her own child, and the smell of roasted meat attracted the hungry who wanted to take the rest from her. These atrocities horrified even the Romans. The dead lay unburied on the roofs of houses, creating a terrible stench in the city – a true apocalyptic image. Although Josephus Flavius tried to convince the Jews to surrender, they hoped for resistance. When the Romans finally breached the walls, Titus, the Roman general, allegedly did not want to burn the Temple, but a soldier threw a torch that caught the interior. The Temple was razed to the ground, and silver and gold were plundered. The Arch of Triumph in Rome still testifies to that event today, depicting the menorah stolen from the Temple. It is interesting that the Temple was destroyed on the same day as the first Temple, which the Babylonians destroyed.

sections.subtitle: Razdvajanje židovstva i kršćanstva: Od religije žrtve do religije Knjige
sections.screenshot_description: Simboličan prikaz razdvajanja židovstva i kršćanstva nakon uništenja Hrama.
sections.content:
Razorenje Hrama 70. godine poslije Krista imalo je dalekosežne posljedice, dovodeći do najjasnijeg razdvajanja židovstva i kršćanstva. Budući da kršćani nisu bili u opkoljenom Jeruzalemu, izbjegli su masakr, što je dodatno naglasilo razliku između dviju zajednica. Nestankom Hrama, prestala je mogućnost prinošenja žrtava, što je prisililo židovstvo na preoblikovanje. Rabini su od religije žrtvovanja stvorili 'religiju Knjige', odnosno Tore.

Frante objašnjava da je odnos s Bogom i pojam domovine preseljen u odnos prema Knjizi, simbolično rečeno, 'u džepu je Hram'. To je značilo da više nije bilo jednog fizičkog mjesta koje bi okupljalo narod, već je Tora postala središte židovskog identiteta i vjere. Ovaj preokret doveo je do definitivnog razdvajanja 'onih koji su na putu' (Kristovih sljedbenika) i 'onih koji su bili protiv Krista'. Euzebije Cezarejski, pišući ranu povijest Crkve, naglašava kako su prvi kršćani razorenje Hrama razumjeli kao ispunjenje Isusovog proročanstva, potvrđujući time Kristovu božansku narav i autoritet.
sections.subtitle_en: Separation of Judaism and Christianity: From the religion of sacrifice to the religion of the Book
sections.screenshot_description_en: Symbolic representation of the separation of Judaism and Christianity after the destruction of the Temple.
sections.content_en: The destruction of the Temple in 70 AD had far-reaching consequences, leading to the clearest separation of Judaism and Christianity. Since Christians were not in besieged Jerusalem, they avoided the massacre, which further emphasized the difference between the two communities. With the disappearance of the Temple, the possibility of offering sacrifices ceased, which forced Judaism to reconfigure itself. Rabbis created from the religion of sacrifice 'the religion of the Book', i.e., the Torah. Frante explains that the relationship with God and the concept of homeland was transferred to the relationship with the Book, symbolically speaking, 'the Temple is in the pocket'. This meant that there was no longer one physical place that would gather the people, but the Torah became the center of Jewish identity and faith. This turning point led to the definitive separation of 'those who are on the way' (Christ's followers) and 'those who were against Christ'. Euzebije Cezarejski, writing the early history of the Church, emphasizes how the first Christians understood the destruction of the Temple as the fulfillment of Jesus' prophecy, thereby confirming Christ's divine nature and authority.

sections.subtitle: Treći Hram i Krist kao novi Hram: Kontroverze i duhovna perspektiva
sections.screenshot_description: Prikaz Hramske gore s Kupolom na stijeni i džamijom Al-Aksa, simbolizirajući napetosti oko Trećeg Hrama.
sections.content:
Voditelj postavlja pitanje o želji Židova za izgradnjom Trećeg Hrama. Frante objašnjava da židovstvo nije jedinstveno po tom pitanju. Postoji struja koja smatra da je dobro što Hrama nema, jer bi obnova žrtvovanja životinja bila neprihvatljiva u modernom svijetu. Za njih, Hram bez oltara i žrtvenika nema smisla. S druge strane, postoji aktivna struja koja teži viziji Trećeg Hrama, čak i kopajući ispod postojećih džamija na Hramskoj gori, što izaziva velike napetosti s islamskim svijetom. Treća struja čak ne prihvaća današnju državu Izrael kao Božju volju.

Frante podsjeća da je Hramsko mjesto povezano s brdom Morija, gdje je Abraham trebao žrtvovati Izaka. Za kršćane, nestanak Hrama nije donio veliku novost, jer je Krist uskrsli Hram. 'Gdje god je Krist, tu je Hram,' objašnjava Frante, misleći na mjesta gdje se ljudi vole, prihvaćaju, opraštaju, slave liturgiju i hrane se njegovim tijelom. To je mjesto Božje prisutnosti, što je duhovna dimenzija prorečena Isusovim riječima, nadilazeći potrebu za fizičkom građevinom.
sections.subtitle_en: Third Temple and Christ as new Temple: Controversies and spiritual perspective
sections.screenshot_description_en: Depiction of the Temple Mount with the Dome of the Rock and Al-Aqsa Mosque, symbolizing tensions around the Third Temple.
sections.content_en: The host asks a question about the Jews' desire for the construction of the Third Temple. Frante explains that Judaism is not unified on this issue. There is a current that believes it is good that there is no Temple, because the renewal of animal sacrifice would be unacceptable in the modern world. For them, a Temple without an altar and sacrificial altar makes no sense. On the other hand, there is an active current that strives for the vision of the Third Temple, even digging beneath existing mosques on the Temple Mount, which causes great tensions with the Islamic world. A third current does not even accept today's state of Israel as God's will. Frante reminds that the Temple site is connected with Mount Moriah, where Abraham was supposed to sacrifice Isaac. For Christians, the disappearance of the Temple did not bring great novelty, because Christ is the risen Temple. 'Wherever Christ is, there is the Temple,' explains Frante, referring to places where people love each other, accept each other, forgive each other, celebrate the liturgy, and feed on His body. That is a place of God's presence, which is a spiritual dimension prophesied by Jesus' words, transcending the need for a physical building.

sections.subtitle: Značenje 'grozote pustoši' i poziv na hitan bijeg
sections.screenshot_description: Prikaz ljudi koji u panici bježe iz grada, ostavljajući sve za sobom.
sections.content:
Voditelj citira Isusove riječi: 'Kad vidite da grozota pustoši stoluje gdje joj nije mjesto... koji se tada zateknu u Judeji neka bježe u gore, tko bude na krovu neka ne silazi i neka ne ulazi u kuću da iz nje što uzme i tko bude u polju neka se ne okreće natrag da uzme ogrtač.' Frante objašnjava da se ova upozorenja odnose na konkretne događaje koji su prethodili opsadi Jeruzalema, poput židovskog ustanka 66. godine i rastućeg neprijateljstva Rimljana.

Poruka je bila jasna: udaljiti se iz Jeruzalema prije nego što bude opkoljen. Kršćani su, prepoznavši znakove, pobjegli u Pelu, izbjegavši tako zarobljeništvo unutar grada. Frante naglašava da u takvim trenucima nije bilo važno posjedovati kuću ili imovinu, već spasiti život. Oni koji su prepoznali nadolazeće zlo i zatvaranje Jeruzalema izvana, imali su priliku otići na sigurno, dok su oni koji su ostali bili zarobljeni u gradu, suočeni s neizrecivim patnjama.
sections.subtitle_en: The meaning of 'abomination of desolation' and a call to urgent flight
sections.screenshot_description_en: A depiction of people who in panic are fleeing from the city, leaving everything behind them.
sections.content_en:
The host quotes Jesus' words: 'When you see the abomination of desolation standing where it ought not... those who are then in Judea must flee to the mountains, whoever is on the housetop must not go down or enter the house to take anything from it and whoever is in the field must not turn back to get his cloak.' Frante explains that these warnings refer to concrete events that preceded the siege of Jeruzalem, such as the Jewish uprising in 66 AD and the growing hostility of the Romans.
The message was clear: to move away from Jeruzalem before it was surrounded. Christians, recognizing the signs, fled to Pela, thus avoiding captivity within the city. Frante emphasizes that in such moments it was not important to own a house or property, but to save one's life. Those who recognized the approaching evil and the external closing of Jeruzalem, had the opportunity to go to safety, while those who remained were trapped in the city, faced with unspeakable sufferings.

sections.subtitle: Psihološki utjecaj gladi: Ljudska okrutnost i sjećanje na patnju
sections.screenshot_description: Prikaz ljudi koji pate od gladi, s izrazima očaja i iscrpljenosti.
sections.content:
Voditelj i Frante razgovaraju o dubokom psihološkom utjecaju gladi i ljudskoj okrutnosti koju ona može izazvati. Frante naglašava da glad tjera ljude na zvjerstva koja inače nikada ne bi počinili. U takvim ekstremnim uvjetima, gubi se razumsko razmišljanje, a prevladava instinkt za preživljavanjem, što dovodi do toga da ljudi 'jedu sve što im dođe pod ruku'.

Frante spominje tradicionalne molitve u Crkvi koje su uključivale zazive za oslobođenje od rata, gladi i kuge, što svjedoči o povijesnoj svijesti o tim strašnim iskustvima. Ljudi koji su preživjeli glad, poput franjevaca iz Drugog svjetskog rata, često su razvili nesvjesni strah i potrebu da uvijek imaju nešto hrane sa strane, čak i godinama kasnije. To je bio instinktivni odgovor na traumu. Frante navodi i primjer selidbe ljudi iz Dalmacije u Slavoniju tijekom Drugog svjetskog rata, gdje je Slavonija, bogata pšenicom, bila 'obećana zemlja' u usporedbi s Dalmacijom gdje nije bilo dovoljno hrane. Ova iskustva pokazuju kako glad može trajno obilježiti pojedince i cijele zajednice.
sections.subtitle_en: Psychological impact of hunger: Human cruelty and memory of suffering
sections.screenshot_description_en: Depiction of people who suffer from hunger, with expressions of despair and exhaustion.
sections.content_en: The host and Frante discuss the deep psychological impact of hunger and the human cruelty it can cause. Frante emphasizes that hunger drives people to atrocities they would otherwise never commit. In such extreme conditions, rational thinking is lost, and the instinct for survival prevails, leading people to 'eat everything that comes their way'. Frante mentions traditional prayers in the Church that included invocations for deliverance from war, hunger, and plague, which testifies to the historical awareness of these terrible experiences. People who survived hunger, like the Franciscans from World War II, often developed an unconscious fear and a need to always have some food aside, even years later. That was an instinctive response to trauma. Frante also cites the example of people migrating from Dalmacija to Slavonija during World War II, where Slavonija, rich in wheat, was 'the promised land' compared to Dalmacija where there was not enough food. These experiences show how hunger can permanently mark individuals and entire communities.

sections.subtitle: Završna poruka: 'Budite na oprezu' i duhovna snaga u kaosu
sections.screenshot_description: Isusov lik s upozoravajućim izrazom, simbolizirajući poziv na budnost i oprez.
sections.content:
Isusova završna poruka u ovom dijelu evanđelja je snažan poziv na budnost i oprez. On ponavlja upozorenja na lažne kriste i lažne proroke koji će činiti čudesa, ali ističe: 'Vi budite na oprezu, evo prorokovao sam vam sve.' Voditelj tumači ovu poruku kao poziv da se ljudi ne prestraše kada se nevolje dogode, već da budu duhovno i materijalno jaki i pripravni.

Frante se slaže, ističući da nitko ne zna kakav će svijet biti sutra. Hrvatska je, za razliku od većine Europe, iskusila Domovinski rat, a mnogi su preživjeli i Drugi svjetski rat, što nam daje svijest o tome kako se sustav može raspasti u kratkom vremenu. Rat prekida normalan tijek života i tjera ljude da svijet promatraju iz drugačije perspektive. U takvim vremenima, ključno je sačuvati odnos s Bogom i Kristom, koji pokazuje da svijet, onako kako ga Bog želi, ima dimenziju koja nadilazi rat. Frante podsjeća da rat, suprotno Heraklitovoj filozofiji, nije 'otac svih stvari'. Važno je osloniti se na Onoga tko je pobijedio zlo i smrt, imajući povjerenja i ostajući budan.
sections.subtitle_en: Final message: 'Be on guard' and spiritual strength in chaos
sections.screenshot_description_en: Jesus' figure with a warning expression, symbolizing a call to vigilance and caution.
sections.content_en: Jesus' final message in this part of the Gospel is a strong call to vigilance and caution. He repeats warnings about false Christs and false prophets who will perform wonders, but emphasizes: 'You be on your guard, behold I have prophesied everything to you.' The host interprets this message as a call for people not to be afraid when troubles occur, but to be spiritually and materially strong and prepared. Frante agrees, pointing out that no one knows what the world will be like tomorrow. Croatia, unlike most of Europe, experienced the Homeland War, and many also survived the Second World War, which gives us an awareness of how a system can collapse in a short time. War interrupts the normal course of life and forces people to view the world from a different perspective. In such times, it is crucial to preserve the relationship with God and Christ, which shows that the world, as God desires it, has a dimension that transcends war. Frante reminds that war, contrary to Heraclitus' philosophy, is not 'the father of all things'. It is important to rely on Him who conquered evil and death, having trust and remaining vigilant.

sections.subtitle: Krhkost mira i važnost vjere u turbulentnim vremenima
sections.screenshot_description: Prikaz globalnih sukoba ili turbulentnih događaja, naglašavajući krhkost mira.
sections.content:
Voditelj zaključuje da Isusove eshatološke besjede nisu depresivne, već oprezne i dramatične poruke namijenjene zrelim ljudima. One nas podsjećaju na krhkost mira i činjenicu da se stvari brzo mijenjaju, te da civilizacije propadaju i dižu se. Ne smijemo se uljuljkati u ideju da smo na vrhuncu civilizacije i da će budućnost donijeti samo napredak.

Frante ističe da je Hrvatska, za razliku od većeg dijela Europe, imala ratove u novijoj povijesti (Domovinski rat, Drugi svjetski rat), što nam daje drugačiju perspektivu. Dok su cijele generacije u Europi mogle živjeti u iluziji da ratova više neće biti, mi znamo da se sukobi mogu pojaviti u trenu. U takvim neizvjesnim vremenima, ključno je biti oslonjen na Onoga tko je pobijedio zlo i smrt. Frante ponavlja Isusovu poruku: 'Vi pazite sami na sebe, na svoje obitelji, svoje bližnje', priznajući da nekada nije moguće promijeniti cijeli svijet, ali je važno sačuvati vlastitu duhovnu snagu i vjeru.
sections.subtitle_en: Fragility of peace and importance of faith in turbulent times
sections.screenshot_description_en: Presentation of global conflicts or turbulent events, emphasizing the fragility of peace.
sections.content_en: The host concludes that Jesus' eschatological discourses are not depressing, but cautious and dramatic messages intended for mature people. They remind us of the fragility of peace and the fact that things change quickly, and that civilizations fall and rise. We must not lull ourselves into the idea that we are at the peak of civilization and that the future will bring only progress. Frante points out that Croatia, unlike most of Europe, has had wars in recent history (Homeland War, Second World War), which gives us a different perspective. While entire generations in Europe could live in the illusion that there would be no more wars, we know that conflicts can appear in an instant. In such uncertain times, it is crucial to rely on Him who conquered evil and death. Frante repeats Jesus' message: 'You take care of yourselves, your families, your loved ones,' acknowledging that sometimes it is not possible to change the whole world, but it is important to preserve one's own spiritual strength and faith.

iterations.theme_en: Eschatological discourses: Warnings, persecutions and the destruction of Jerusalem
